#Text=_Poisonous Pickles._
#Text=
#Text=
#Text=Vegetable substances, preserved in the state called pickles, by means of
#Text=the antiseptic power of vinegar, whose sale frequently depends greatly
#Text=upon a fine lively green colour; and the consumption of which, by
#Text=sea-faring people in particular, is prodigious, are sometimes
#Text=intentionally coloured by means of copper.
#Text=Gerkins, French beans,
#Text=samphires, the green pods of capsicum, and many other pickled vegetable
#Text=substances, oftener than is perhaps expected, are met with impregnated
#Text=with this metal.
#Text=Numerous fatal consequences are known to have ensued
#Text=from the use of these stimulants of the palate, to which the fresh and
#Text=pleasing hue has been imparted according to the deadly _formulæ_ laid
#Text=down in some modern cookery books, such as boiling the pickles with
#Text=half-pence, or suffering them to stand for a considerable period in
#Text=brazen vessels.
#Text=Dr.
#Text=Percival[106] has given an account of "a young lady who amused
#Text=herself, while her hair was dressing, with eating samphire pickles
#Text=impregnated with copper.

#Text=_To make greening._[108]--"Take a bit of verdigris, the bigness of a

column1: #Text=hazel-nut, finely powdered; half-a-pint of distilled vinegar, and a bit

column1: #Text=halfpence, or allow them to stand for twenty-four hours in copper or
#Text=brass pans."
#Text=To detect the presence of copper, it is only necessary to mince the
#Text=pickles, and to pour liquid ammonia, diluted with an equal bulk of
#Text=water, over them in a stopped phial: if the pickles contain the minutest
#Text=quantity of copper, the ammonia assumes a blue colour.
#Text=SPECIFIC DIFFERENCES, AND COMPONENT PARTS OF WINE.
#Text=Every body knows that no product of the arts varies so much as wine;
#Text=that different countries, and sometimes the different provinces of the
#Text=same country, produce different wines.
#Text=These differences, no doubt, must
#Text=be attributed chiefly to the climate in which the vineyard is
#Text=situated--to its culture--the quantity of sugar contained in the grape
#Text=juice--the manufacture of the wine; or the mode of suffering its
#Text=fermentation to be accomplished.
#Text=If the grapes be gathered unripe, the
#Text=wine abounds with acid; but if the fruit be gathered ripe, the wine will
#Text=be rich.
#Text=When the proportion of sugar in the grape is sufficient, and
#Text=the fermentation complete, the wine is perfect and generous.
#Text=If the
#Text=quantity of sugar be too large, part of it remains undecomposed, as the
#Text=fermentation is languid, and the wine is sweet and luscious; if, on the
#Text=contrary, it contains, even when full ripe, only a small portion of
#Text=sugar, the wine is thin and weak; and if it be bottled before the
#Text=fermentation be completed, part of the sugar remains undecomposed, the
#Text=fermentation will go on slowly in the bottle, and, on drawing the cork,
#Text=the wine sparkles in the glass; as, for example, Champagne.
#Text=Such wines
#Text=are not sufficiently mature.
#Text=When the must is separated from the husk of
#Text=the red grape before it is fermented, the wine has little or no colour:
#Text=these are called _white_ wines.
#Text=If, on the contrary, the husks are
#Text=allowed to remain in the must while the fermentation is going on, the
#Text=alcohol dissolves the colouring matter of the husks, and the wine is
#Text=coloured: such are called _red_ wines.
#Text=Hence white wines are often
#Text=prepared from red grapes, the liquor being drawn off before it has
#Text=acquired the red colour; for the skin of the grape only gives the
#Text=colour.
#Text=Besides in these principal circumstances, wines vary much in
#Text=flavour.
#Text=All wines contain one common and identical principle, from which their
#Text=similar effects are produced; namely, _brandy_ or _alcohol_.
#Text=It is
#Text=especially by the different proportions of brandy contained in wines,
#Text=that they differ most from one another.
#Text=When wine is distilled, the
#Text=alcohol readily separates.
#Text=The spirit thus obtained is well known under
#Text=the name of _brandy_.
#Text=_Counterfeit Pepper._
#Text=
#Text=
#Text=Black pepper is the fruit of a shrubby creeping plant, which grows wild
#Text=in the East Indies, and is cultivated, with much advantage, for the sake
#Text=of its berries, in Java and Malabar.
#Text=The berries are gathered before
#Text=they are ripe, and are dried in the sun.
#Text=They become black and
#Text=corrugated on the surface.
#Text=This factitious pepper-corns have of late been detected mixed with
#Text=genuine pepper, is a fact sufficiently known.[104] Such an adulteration
#Text=may prove, in many instances of household economy, exceedingly vexatious
#Text=and prejudicial to those who ignorantly make use of the spurious
#Text=article.
#Text=I have examined large packages of both black and white pepper,
#Text=by order of the Excise, and have found them to contain about 16 per
#Text=cent. of this artificial compound.
#Text=The spurious pepper is made up of
#Text=oil cakes (the residue of lintseed, from which the oil has been
#Text=pressed,) common clay, and a portion of Cayenne pepper, formed in a
#Text=mass, and granulated by being first pressed through a sieve, and then
#Text=rolled in a cask.
#Text=The mode of detecting the fraud is easy.
#Text=It is only
#Text=necessary to throw a sample of the suspected pepper into a bowl of
#Text=water; the artificial pepper-corns fall to powder, whilst the true
#Text=pepper remains whole.
#Text=Ground pepper is very often sophisticated by adding to a portion of
#Text=genuine pepper, a quantity of pepper dust, or the sweepings from the
#Text=pepper warehouses, mixed with a little Cayenne pepper.
#Text=The sweepings are
#Text=known, and purchased in the market, under the name of P.
#Text=D. signifying
#Text=pepper dust.
#Text=An inferior sort of this vile refuse, or the sweepings of
#Text=P.
#Text=D. is distinguished among venders by the abbreviation of D.
#Text=P.
#Text=D.
#Text=denoting, dust (dirt) of pepper dust.
#Text=The adulteration of pepper, and the making and selling commodities in
#Text=imitation of pepper, are prohibited, under a severe penalty.
#Text=The
#Text=following are the words of the Act:[105]
#Text=
#Text="And whereas commodities made in imitation of pepper have of late been
#Text=sold and found in the possession of various dealers in pepper, and other
#Text=persons in Great Britain; be it therefore enacted, that from and after
#Text=the said 5th day of July, 1819, if any commodity or substance shall be
#Text=prepared by any person in imitation of pepper, shall be mixed with
#Text=pepper, or sold or delivered as and for, or as a substitute for, pepper,
#Text=or if any such commodity or substance, alone or mixed, shall be kept for
#Text=sale, sold, or delivered, or shall be offered or exposed to sale, or
#Text=shall be in the custody or possession of any dealer or seller of pepper,
#Text=the same, together with all pepper with which the same shall be mixed,
#Text=shall be forfeited, with the packages containing the same, and shall and
#Text=may be seized by any officer of excise; and the person preparing,
#Text=manufacturing, mixing as aforesaid, selling, exposing to sale, or
#Text=delivering the same, or having the same in his, her, or their custody or
#Text=possession, shall forfeit the sum of one hundred pounds."
#Text=WHITE PEPPER.
#Text=The common white pepper is factitious, being prepared from the black
#Text=pepper in the following manner:--The pepper is first steeped in sea
#Text=water and urine, and then exposed to the heat of the sun for several
#Text=days, till the rind or outer bark loosens; it is then taken out of the
#Text=steep, and, when dry, it is rubbed with the hand till the rind falls
#Text=off.
#Text=The white fruit is then dried, and the remains of the rind blown
#Text=away like chaff.
#Text=A great deal of the peculiar flavour and pungent hot
#Text=taste of the pepper is taken off by this process.
#Text=White pepper is always
#Text=inferior in flavour and quality to the black pepper.
#Text=However, there is a sort of native white pepper, produced on a species
#Text=of the pepper plant, which is much better than the factitious, and
#Text=indeed little inferior to the common black pepper.
#Text=_Poisonous Cayenne Pepper._
#Text=
#Text=
#Text=Cayenne pepper is an indiscriminate mixture of the powder of the dried
#Text=pods of many species of capsicum, but especially of the capsicum
#Text=frutescens, or bird pepper, which is the hottest of all.
#Text=This annual plant, a native of South America, is cultivated in large
#Text=quantities in our West-India islands, and even frequently in our
#Text=gardens, for the beauty of its pods, which are long, pointed, and
#Text=pendulous, at first of a green colour, and, when ripe, of a bright
#Text=orange red.
#Text=They are filled with a dry loose pulp, and contain many
#Text=small, flat, kidney-shaped seeds.
#Text=The taste of capsicum is extremely
#Text=pungent and acrimonious, setting the mouth, as it were, on fire.
#Text=The principle on which its pungency depends, is soluble in water and in
#Text=alcohol.
#Text=It is sometimes adulterated with red lead, to prevent it becoming
#Text=bleached on exposure to light.
#Text=This fraud may be readily detected by
#Text=shaking up part of it in a stopped vial containing water impregnated
#Text=with sulphuretted hydrogen gas, which will cause it speedily to assume a
#Text=dark muddy black colour.
#Text=Or the vegetable matter of the pepper may be
#Text=destroyed, by throwing a mixture of one part of the suspected pepper and
#Text=three of nitrate of potash (or two of chlorate of potash) into a red-hot
#Text=crucible, in small quantities at a time.
#Text=The mass left behind may then
#Text=be digested in weak nitric acid, and the solution assayed for lead by
#Text=water impregnated with sulphuretted hydrogen.
